messages: I keep meaning to learn how to make a decent vinaigrette; it seems basic but every time it tastes a bit off.
Also curious about buying a cast-iron skillet—I've heard it's versatile but heavy and requires maintenance.
I was thinking of trying a class sometime, but schedules are tight and the ones nearby focus on fancy baking.
Once I tried to time a stew while also catching a livestream, and the timing completely derailed.
I sometimes follow recipes that call for 'to taste' and that phrase annoys me; I prefer more concrete guidance.
Oh, and my cat likes to jump on the counter, so I'm picky about keeping raw ingredients covered—maybe that's paranoia.
Alright, putting the other stuff aside, can you draft a short routine I can actually follow starting this week?
Keep it realistic for weeknights when I only have time roughly the length of a TV episode, and assume I'm working with a modest home kitchen.
I'd like the emphasis to be on transferable basics—better cutting technique, managing how things brown, a couple of simple pan sauces, and putting quick dinners together.
Favor ingredients I can buy at a normal supermarket without hunting down specialty stores.
For each session, give a concise objective and one practical drill, plus one quick way to check if I'm getting better.
Please keep each day's instructions short and actionable—no long essays.
If a couple of brief meal examples help clarify a drill, include them, but don't give full long recipes.
Also list any small, inexpensive items worth picking up before starting the routine.
Could you map that into a compact schedule I can follow over the next couple of weeks?
Finally, suggest one tiny daily habit I can adopt so the skills stick beyond the practice sessions.
